danger_signals.visual_form: Animated hostile sprites in underwater environments
danger_signals.meaning: Threat to player progression; requires bubble-trapping mechanic to neutralize

danger_signals.visual_form: Six escalating stage themes (Coral Sea → Sunken Ship → Sea of Ice → Undersea Volcano → Ursula's Castle)
danger_signals.meaning: Increasing difficulty and danger as player approaches final confrontation with Ursula

danger_signals.visual_form: Stage-specific visual design (volcanic heat, ice formations, ship wreckage)
danger_signals.meaning: Environmental context signals thematic danger aligned with stage setting

safety_signals.meaning: Safe recovery mechanic; visual indicator of resource availability
Safety buffer against failure; visual representation of player resilience

safety_signals.visual_form: Sea shells opening treasure chests containing bubble upgrades
safety_signals.meaning: Safe progression mechanism; digging and collection rewards increase player capability

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Underwater kingdom traversal; royal heritage implied through narrative context

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Castle location as seat of power; witchcraft/dark magic implied
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Final two-stage boss battle at Ursula's Castle
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Castle setting as opposing faction stronghold; serves as narrative destination

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Coral Sea stage with peaceful, colorful aquatic environments
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Ariel descends from top of screen to recommended starting position (NES version); gentle introduction to mechanics

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Sunken Ship, Sea of Ice, Undersea Volcano stages with increasingly foreboding aesthetics
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemy density and difficulty escalates; stage backgrounds become more restrictive/claustrophobic

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Two-stage castle boss encounter; spatial confinement contrasts with open underwater stages
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Boss-specific battle patterns; final narrative checkpoint before ending

environmental_storytelling.example: Sunken Ship represents historical threat/decay; Sea of Ice suggests frozen/hostile realm; Undersea Volcano indicates approaching Ursula's dark domain
NES version features more detailed stage backgrounds than Game Boy version, creating depth and spatial storytelling about underwater world structure
Digging through sand to find treasure establishes player agency in world-state interaction; shell-opening mechanic ties environmental objects to progression

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Can shoot bubbles, trap enemies, dig through sand, collect shells and power-ups; responsive to player input
Heart icon transforms to heart frame (NES) or disappears (Game Boy); visual health indicator diminishes
Patrol underwater environments in predictable patterns; threat until engaged by player bubble mechanic
Trapped in bubbles and thrown; removed from stage; visual removal confirms successful action
Two-stage castle battle with unique attack patterns; boss-specific music (different key scale between NES/GB versions) reinforces narrative weight

notes: The Little Mermaid NES uses straightforward visual communication aligned with Disney film narrative. No complex faction systems present; antagonism is binary (Ariel vs. Ursula). Visual storytelling relies heavily on stage-based theming and color/environment contrast. The game employs a clear danger-progression model where stage difficulty escalates spatially toward Ursula's Castle. Manifest gameplay mechanics (bubble-trapping, treasure-hunting) serve as core visual language for world interaction. Notable platform difference: Game Boy version uses more restricted backgrounds, creating distinct visual manifestation hierarchy between versions.
